title: Homological versus Hodge-theoretic mirror symmetry
speaker: Timothy Perutz
affiliation: University of Texas, Austin; von Neumann Fellow, School of Mathematics
abstract: I'll describe joint work with Sheel Ganatra and Nick Sheridan which rigorously establishes the relationship between different aspects of the mirror symmetry phenomenon for Calabi-Yau manifolds. Homological mirror symmetry---an abstract, categorical statement---implies Hodge theoretic mirror symmetry, a concrete relation between counts of rational curves and variations of Hodge structure.

proof_stages.label: HMS-to-closed-string theorem and the quantum operator
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up a smooth projective Calabi-Yau X as a symplectic manifold and states the main result: homological mirror symmetry for X and a mirror Y over a Laurent-series field implies that the quantum product operator R, encoded by genus-zero three-point Gromov-Witten invariants of X, is interpretable in the Hodge theory of Y.

proof_stages.label: Hodge-theoretic target via Yukawa/Gauss-Manin data
proof_stages.summary: The closed-string generating series is described on the mirror side as a Yukawa coupling obtained from a holomorphic volume form and a Gauss-Manin-type connection, with the mirror coordinate and volume form normalized by leading terms and monodromy-weight behavior.

proof_stages.label: Expected SYZ/HMS-to-Hodge pipeline
proof_stages.summary: The theorem is placed in the broader expected mirror-symmetry picture: a maximally degenerate Calabi-Yau should produce a Lagrangian torus fibration and an intrinsic rigid-analytic mirror for which HMS is natural, after which the present implication should recover Hodge-theoretic Gromov-Witten predictions.

proof_stages.label: Fukaya-category input from Floer theory
proof_stages.summary: The A-side category is built by forming Floer complexes from Lagrangian intersections, defining differentials by counts of index-one holomorphic strips with Novikov area weights, addressing gradings and bubbling issues, and assembling the higher A-infinity operations from holomorphic polygons before passing to a split-closed dg enlargement.

proof_stages.label: Relative Fukaya category over the Laurent-series field
proof_stages.summary: To obtain an algebraic model over k=C((q)), one chooses an ample divisor D, restricts to exact Lagrangians in X\D, weights holomorphic polygons by intersection with D, and formulates relative HMS as a quasi-equivalence between the enlarged relative Fukaya category and perfect complexes on a smooth projective Calabi-Yau mirror Y.

proof_stages.label: Smoothness and open-closed reconstruction of quantum cohomology
proof_stages.summary: Relative HMS supplies dg-categorical smoothness, and the key A-side theorem says that a smooth nonempty relative Fukaya category determines closed-string quantum cohomology and its structures through Hochschild, cohomological, and periodic cyclic invariants, in particular via an isomorphism from Hochschild homology to quantum cohomology.

proof_stages.label: Transfer to B-side Hodge theory and extraction of R
proof_stages.summary: The quasi-equivalence with Perf(Y) identifies quantum cohomology of X with algebraic cohomology of Y while matching the quantum differential operator, whose R-term is the original genus-zero Gromov-Witten operator, with Getzler's Gauss-Manin connection and hence with the mirror variation of Hodge structure.

examples.name: Flops between possible mirrors
examples.note: In response to a uniqueness question, flops are mentioned as an illustrative way in which a mirror satisfying the categorical characterization need not be unique, without supplying any proof ingredient.
examples.engagement_reason: flops are only cited as a possible non-uniqueness relating different mirrors, with no analysis of a flop

Flops are ordinary birational ambiguities between Calabi–Yau mirrors and are invoked to show uniqueness of the mirror fails.

used to show the mirror satisfying HMS need not be unique, with flops being wall/extremal-ray birational moves.

invoked to show that the HMS mirror need not be unique, while flops are standard birational moves

examples.name: Quintic threefold and its standard mirror
examples.note: The quintic threefold in CP^4 is used as the concrete test case where Sheridan's HMS theorem and the HMS-implies-Hodge result recover the classical Yukawa-coupling formula for the Gromov-Witten series.
examples.engagement_reason: the quintic and its standard mirror are used to state Sheridan’s HMS result, form the Gromov-Witten generating series, give initial terms, and identify it with a Yukawa/Gauss-Manin expression

passes.reason: the smooth quintic and its standard mirror are used as the main illustrative HMS example, not as a structural exception or obstruction
The quintic threefold with its standard mirror is a classic representative Calabi–Yau mirror pair used as a positive test case.
The quintic threefold with its standard mirror is used as the main illustrative case of HMS implying the Yukawa/Gromov-Witten formula, not as a failure case.
the quintic mirror pair is used as a standard positive test case of the theorem
The quintic Calabi–Yau threefold with its standard mirror is a central but systematic member of a mirror family, used as a positive test case.
the quintic threefold with its standard mirror is used as the main illustrative case of the theorem, not as a failure case, and is a standard Calabi-Yau example rather than structurally exceptional
used as the standard benchmark illustrating the theorem and the quintic mirror formula, not as an obstruction or sporadic object.
It is the main illustrative Calabi-Yau mirror pair for the theorem, and the quintic belongs to a systematic hypersurface family.
used as a positive test case for the theorem, with the quintic/mirror treated as a standard systematic Calabi–Yau example

used as the standard positive mirror-symmetry test case, a systematic Calabi-Yau hypersurface and mirror family

examples.engagement_reason: K3 surfaces are only invoked via the known derived Torelli theorem as a case where one might go backwards

examples.name: Two transverse Lagrangians in R^2
examples.note: The planar picture of two transverse Lagrangians makes concrete why the Floer chain complex is generated by intersection points, but the construction is not logically dependent on this local drawing.
examples.engagement_reason: the transverse Lagrangians picture is used to build the Floer complex from intersection points and describe the differential by counting holomorphic strips
